Introduction to DECODE

r_dir <- # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
    # "/Users/kndinh/RESEARCH AND EVERYTHING/Projects/GITHUB/DECODE/R"
    "/Users/yanjiechen/Documents/Github/DECODE/R"
r_files <- list.files( # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
    r_dir,
    pattern = "[.]r$|[.]R$", full.names = TRUE
)
invisible(lapply(r_files, source)) # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
# library(DECODE) # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< UNCOMMENT WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
set.seed(1)

DECODE (Deciphering Cancer Origin from DNA Evolution) decomposes mutations from a bulk DNA-sequencing sample into a neutral tail and mutation clusters, based on their Variant Allele Frequencies (VAFs). The algorithm corrects for sample-specific sequencing coverage and mutation-calling biases when computing the expected Site Frequency Spectrum (SFS).

This vignette walks through a complete hands-on workflow:

  • Section 1: Prepare a mutation table with annotated copy numbers
  • Section 2: Decompose diploid mutations with DECODE
  • Section 3: Generate diagnostic plots
  • Section 4: Extract key biological parameters
  • Section 5: Assign mutations to DECODE compartments

1. Prepare a mutation table with annotated copy numbers

DECODE requires a mutation table with columns Ref_count and Alt_count, containing the reference and alternative read counts for each mutation, respectively.

# mutation_table <- read.csv( # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< UNCOMMENT WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
#     system.file("extdata", "mutation_table.csv", package = "DECODE")
# )
mutation_table <- # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
    # read.csv("/Users/kndinh/RESEARCH AND EVERYTHING/Projects/GITHUB/DECODE/inst/extdata/mutation_table.csv")
    read.csv("/Users/yanjiechen/Documents/Github/DECODE/inst/extdata/mutation_table.csv")
head(mutation_table)
#>   Mutation_ID Chromosome Position Ref_count Alt_count
#> 1  mutation_1       chr1   117291        72        33
#> 2  mutation_2       chr1   481356       122        45
#> 3  mutation_3       chr1   949803       163        12
#> 4  mutation_4       chr1  1102170       122        34
#> 5  mutation_5       chr1  1530100       162        11
#> 6  mutation_6       chr1  1564250        99        57

NOTE FOR YANJIE: INCLUDE COLUMN Mutation_ID AS 1ST COLUMN.

Since DECODE operates on the mutational SFS, decomposition should be limited to mutations sharing the same copy-number state, in which case mutation clusters correspond directly to distinct subclones. In practice, DECODE works best for samples that are mainly diploid. To extract only diploid mutations, we first load the table containing allele-specific copy number segments, e.g., from ASCAT.

# cn_table <- read.delim( # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< UNCOMMENT WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
#     system.file("extdata", "copy_number_segments.txt", package = "DECODE")
# )
cn_table <- # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
    # read.delim("/Users/kndinh/RESEARCH AND EVERYTHING/Projects/GITHUB/DECODE/inst/extdata/copy_number_segments.txt")
    read.delim("/Users/yanjiechen/Documents/Github/DECODE/inst/extdata/copy_number_segments.txt")
head(cn_table)
#>   chr  startpos    endpos nMajor nMinor
#> 1   1     61735  10427928      2      1
#> 2   1  10428257  10521313      2      1
#> 3   1  10521990  26613862      1      1
#> 4   1  26614190  27193445      2      0
#> 5   1  27193722 151886189      2      1
#> 6   1 151886412 152106520      1      1

We can then assign allele-specific copy numbers to each mutation.

annotate_mutations_with_cn <- function(mutation_table, cn_table) {
    cn_table$chr <- paste0("chr", cn_table$chr)
    mutation_table$nMajor <- NA_integer_
    mutation_table$nMinor <- NA_integer_
    for (segment in 1:nrow(cn_table)) {
        mutation_rows <- which(
            mutation_table$Chromosome == cn_table$chr[segment] &
                mutation_table$Position >= cn_table$startpos[segment] &
                mutation_table$Position <= cn_table$endpos[segment]
        )
        mutation_table$nMajor[mutation_rows] <- cn_table$nMajor[segment]
        mutation_table$nMinor[mutation_rows] <- cn_table$nMinor[segment]
    }
    return(mutation_table)
}
mutation_table <- annotate_mutations_with_cn(
    mutation_table = mutation_table,
    cn_table = cn_table
)
cat("Total mutations:                     ", nrow(mutation_table), "\nMutations with assigned copy numbers:", sum(!is.na(mutation_table$nMajor)), "\n")
#> Total mutations:                      14187 
#> Mutations with assigned copy numbers: 13981
head(mutation_table)
#>   Mutation_ID Chromosome Position Ref_count Alt_count nMajor nMinor
#> 1  mutation_1       chr1   117291        72        33      2      1
#> 2  mutation_2       chr1   481356       122        45      2      1
#> 3  mutation_3       chr1   949803       163        12      2      1
#> 4  mutation_4       chr1  1102170       122        34      2      1
#> 5  mutation_5       chr1  1530100       162        11      2      1
#> 6  mutation_6       chr1  1564250        99        57      2      1

If allele-specific copy numbers are not available, we can filter for mutations with total copy number = 2. In this case, attention should be paid to potentially spurious clusters. For instance, mutations with (nMajor, nMinor) = (2, 0) may produce secondary clusters with higher VAF compared to their counterparts with (nMajor, nMinor) = (1, 1). These artificial clusters may inflate the clone count and sample purity.

2. Decompose diploid mutations with DECODE

We extract diploid mutations, i.e., those with (nMajor, nMinor) = (1, 1), for DECODE analysis. Furthermore, mutations without reported reference or alternate read counts must be excluded. Optionally, mutations with no reference or alternate read counts can also be omitted.

mutation_table <- mutation_table[which(
    mutation_table$nMajor == 1 &
        mutation_table$nMinor == 1 &
        !is.na(mutation_table$Ref_count) &
        !is.na(mutation_table$Alt_count) &
        mutation_table$Ref_count > 0 &
        mutation_table$Alt_count > 0
), ]
cat("Mutations on diploid regions:        ", nrow(mutation_table), "\n")
#> Mutations on diploid regions:         9381
head(mutation_table)
#>      Mutation_ID Chromosome Position Ref_count Alt_count nMajor nMinor
#> 103 mutation_103       chr1 10577266       144         3      1      1
#> 104 mutation_104       chr1 10627844        60        65      1      1
#> 105 mutation_105       chr1 10710444        74        57      1      1
#> 106 mutation_106       chr1 10793340       121        12      1      1
#> 107 mutation_107       chr1 10962862       119         3      1      1
#> 108 mutation_108       chr1 11097363        69        42      1      1

DECODE requires a sufficient number of mutations to decompose the SFS reliably. If there are fewer than 100 mutations remaining in the table, consider using a different copy number state instead. Note that the ploidy assumption should be accounted for when translating cluster VAFs into subclonal CCFs.

We can then decompose the retained mutations with DECODE(). At minimum, DECODE requires mutation_table, a dataframe with columns Ref_count and Alt_count. DECODE also accepts several optional arguments:

  • sample_id: label for the sample.
  • neutral_tail: whether the decomposition should include a neutral tail (if TRUE) or not (if FALSE). By default, neutral_tail = NA, in which case DECODE will compare the fits with and without a neutral tail to select the parsimonious model.
  • N_clusters: requested cluster count. By default, N_clusters = NA, in which case DECODE will automatically determine the optimal number of clusters.
  • N_clusters_min, N_clusters_max: range of cluster counts (N_clusters_min = 1 and N_clusters_max = 5 by default).
  • ABCSMCDRF_nParticles: a vector of ABC-SMC-DRF particle count (rep(1000, 10) by default). The length of ABCSMCDRF_nParticles indicates the total number of iterations, and each element indicates the particle count for the corresponding iteration. For complicated samples, increasing the iteration count or particle counts may improve DECODE’s accuracy.
DECODE_result <- DECODE(
    sample_id = "Example",
    mutation_table = mutation_table
)
#> 
#> 
#> 
#> PERFORMING DECODE FOR SAMPLE Example...
#> Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.5.2
#> Warning: package 'data.table' was built under R version 4.5.2
#> Choose mutation thresholds for inference and validation...
#>   |                                                          |                                                  |   0%  |                                                          |                 |+                                                 |   2%  |                                                          |+                                                 |   3%  | #> Cluster 1              : f   = 0.43 ± 0.00; mutation count: 4417 ± 6 [True], 4398 ± 6 [A], 4398 ± 6 [B] 
#> Cluster 2              : f   = 0.30 ± 0.00; mutation count: 1239 ± 12 [True], 1234 ± 12 [A], 1234 ± 12 [B] 
#> Cluster 3              : f   = 0.17 ± 0.00; mutation count: 538 ± 2 [True], 535 ± 2 [A], 534 ± 2 [B] 
#> Cluster 4              : f   = 0.09 ± 0.00; mutation count: 797 ± 2 [True], 773 ± 3 [A], 714 ± 4 [B] 
#> Cluster 5              : f   = 0.04 ± 0.00; mutation count: 1548 ± 19 [True], 851 ± 1 [A], 364 ± 4 [B] 
#> H = 5 vs H = 4         : Wilcoxon p-value = 0 → maximum cluster count has been reached, increased N_clusters_max may improve fit
#> With vs without tail   : Wilcoxon p-value = 1 → fit with tail is selected
#> Best fit = neutral tail + 2 clusters:
#> Validation score       : GIC = 0.07 ± 0 (L1 error = 0.01 ± 0, penalty = 0.06 ± 0)
#> Neutral component      : α   = 1.20 ± 0.00; mutation count: 10758 ± 1 [True], 2718 ± 0 [A], 2139 ± 0 [B] 
#> Cluster 1              : f   = 0.44 ± 0.00; mutation count: 4270 ± 0 [True], 4252 ± 0 [A], 4252 ± 0 [B] 
#> Cluster 2              : f   = 0.30 ± 0.00; mutation count: 886 ± 0 [True], 882 ± 0 [A], 882 ± 0 [B]

3. Generate diagnostic plots

DECODE provides two main plotting functions: DECODE_plot_SFS() and DECODE_plot_model_selection(). Both accept optional filename, width, height, units, and res arguments. The defaults are width = 30, height = 15, units = "in", and res = 150 (i.e. 4500×2250 pixels).

DECODE_plot_SFS() compares the empirical SFS against DECODE’s result for particular subsample and fit. By default, it plots the best DECODE fit for the inference A subsample. The user can change to a different subsample with argument mode (which accepts "inference_A", "inference_B" or "validation") and a different fit by changing arguments with_tail ("TRUE" or "FALSE") and N_clusters (any integer cluster count that DECODE considered).

DECODE_plot_SFS(
    DECODE_result = DECODE_result,
    filename = "DECODE_SFS",
    filetype = "png"
)

DECODE_plot_model_selection() provides all information regarding the model selection process.

DECODE_plot_model_selection(
    DECODE_result = DECODE_result,
    filename = "DECODE_model_selection",
    filetype = "png"
)

Top left: heatmap detailing the proportion of mutations retained for given variant and total read count thresholds. Filtering thresholds for inference A, inference B and validation subsamples are highlighted. Right: Optimal decompositions given cluster count \(H\) with a tail (column T+\(H\)) or without a tail (column NT+\(H\)). For each \(H\), the expected SFS for each subsample given the inferred parameters (shaded areas, colors denoting tail and clusters) is compared against the empirical SFS (black histogram). Means and standard deviations of tail power (\(\alpha\)) and cluster VAFs (\(f\)) are reported on top. Bottom left: comparison of Generalized Information Criteria (GIC) between different tail and cluster count assumptions. The fit with the lowest GIC is selected as the final result, and denoted with purple GIC bar and purple box around the corresponding SFS fits.

NOTE FOR YANJIE: CHECK WHY THE TEXTS IN HEATMAP ARE NOT DISPLAYING.

4. Extract key biological parameters

Relevant biological parameters can be extracted or inferred from the output argument DECODE_result.

We first find details regarding DECODE’s best fit, i.e., whether the tail can be detected, the number of clusters, and the corresponding parameter posterior distribution.

with_tail <- DECODE_result$best_with_tail
N_clusters <- DECODE_result$best_N_clusters
fit_key <- ifelse(with_tail, "fits_with_tail", "fits_without_tail")
parameters <- DECODE_result[[fit_key]]$all_fits[[paste0(N_clusters, "_clusters")]]$parameters
cat("DECODE's tail status:                ", with_tail, "\nDECODE's cluster count:              ", N_clusters, "\n")
#> DECODE's tail status:                 TRUE 
#> DECODE's cluster count:               2
head(parameters)
#>   Tail_power Tail_Nmut_exact Tail_Nmut_A Tail_Nmut_B Tail_Nmut_validation
#> 1   1.202059        10758.01    2718.360    2139.324             2389.285
#> 2   1.201973        10756.59    2718.582    2139.552             2389.514
#> 3   1.202103        10758.80    2718.268    2139.226             2389.188
#> 4   1.201973        10756.59    2718.582    2139.552             2389.514
#> 5   1.202059        10758.01    2718.360    2139.324             2389.285
#> 6   1.201973        10756.59    2718.582    2139.552             2389.514
#>   Cluster_1_freq Cluster_1_Nmut_exact Cluster_1_Nmut_A Cluster_1_Nmut_B
#> 1      0.4351267             4269.716         4251.510         4251.510
#> 2      0.4351280             4269.849         4251.643         4251.643
#> 3      0.4351264             4269.698         4251.492         4251.492
#> 4      0.4351280             4269.849         4251.643         4251.643
#> 5      0.4351267             4269.716         4251.510         4251.510
#> 6      0.4351280             4269.849         4251.643         4251.643
#>   Cluster_1_Nmut_validation Cluster_2_freq Cluster_2_Nmut_exact
#> 1                  4251.510      0.2996298             885.6289
#> 2                  4251.643      0.2996581             886.2510
#> 3                  4251.492      0.2996609             886.2122
#> 4                  4251.643      0.2996581             886.2510
#> 5                  4251.510      0.2996298             885.6289
#> 6                  4251.643      0.2996581             886.2510
#>   Cluster_2_Nmut_A Cluster_2_Nmut_B Cluster_2_Nmut_validation
#> 1         881.8521         881.8410                  881.8499
#> 2         882.4716         882.4604                  882.4693
#> 3         882.4329         882.4218                  882.4306
#> 4         882.4716         882.4604                  882.4693
#> 5         881.8521         881.8410                  881.8499
#> 6         882.4716         882.4604                  882.4693

4a. Sample purity and subclonal Cancer Cell Fractions (CCFs)

DECODE estimates the clusters’ mean VAFs (reported in columns Cluster_i_freq in parameters). The formula for translating the VAF for clone \(i\) into its subclonal CCF requires knowing sample purity \(\rho\), copy number \(N\) and multiplicity \(m\):

\[\mathrm{CCF}_i \approx \frac{\mathrm{VAF}_i}{m\cdot\rho}\left(\rho\cdot N+2(1-\rho)\right)\]

If DECODE is limited to diploid mutations ((nMajor, nMinor) = (1, 1)), the multiplicity is necessarily 1, and the formula simplifies considerably:

\[\mathrm{CCF}_i \approx \frac{2\cdot\mathrm{VAF}_i}{\rho}\]

The subclonal CCFs, unnormalized for purity, are hence twice the cluster VAFs, averaged among DECODE’s estimates.

cluster_VAFs <- vapply(
    seq_len(N_clusters),
    function(i) mean(parameters[[paste0("Cluster_", i, "_freq")]]),
    numeric(1)
)
cluster_CCFs <- 2 * cluster_VAFs
data.frame(
    Compartment = paste0("Cluster ", seq_len(N_clusters)),
    VAF = cluster_VAFs,
    CCF = cluster_CCFs
)
#>   Compartment       VAF       CCF
#> 1   Cluster 1 0.4351272 0.8702543
#> 2   Cluster 2 0.2996398 0.5992796

DECODE automatically orders the clusters in decreasing VAFs. Therefore, the sample purity equals the unnormalized CCF of cluster 1. Optionally, the subclonal CCFs can then be normalized by dividing by purity.

purity <- cluster_CCFs[1]
purity
#> [1] 0.8702543

The formulae above can be adjusted if a different copy number state from diploidy was employed for DECODE.

4b. Subclonal mutation burden

DECODE does not decompose all mutations in the input table. Instead, it creates three subsamples with varying readcount thresholds:

Subsample Note
inference_A Lowest alternative readcount threshold; if the tail is detected, this subsample retains the most mutations in the tail
inference_B Highest alternative readcount threshold; if the tail is detected, this subsample retains the fewest mutations in the tail
validation Intermediate readcount threshold; mainly used to validate the parameters inferred from inference_A and inference_B

The mutation burden for each compartment, both mutation clusters and the tail if detected by DECODE, in each subsample can be estimated by averaging among DECODE’s estimates.

cluster_burden <- data.frame(
    Compartment = c(ifelse(with_tail, "Tail", c()), paste0("Cluster ", seq_len(N_clusters))),
    CCF = c(ifelse(with_tail, c(NA), c()), cluster_CCFs),
    nMut = c(sum(DECODE_result$SFS_data_inference_A), sum(DECODE_result$SFS_data_inference_B), sum(DECODE_result$SFS_data_validation)),
    nMut_inference_A = c(
        ifelse(with_tail, round(mean(parameters[["Tail_Nmut_A"]])), c()),
        vapply(seq_len(N_clusters), function(i) round(mean(parameters[[paste0("Cluster_", i, "_Nmut_A")]])), numeric(1))
    ),
    nMut_inference_B = c(
        ifelse(with_tail, round(mean(parameters[["Tail_Nmut_B"]])), c()),
        vapply(seq_len(N_clusters), function(i) round(mean(parameters[[paste0("Cluster_", i, "_Nmut_B")]])), numeric(1))
    ),
    nMut_validation = c(
        ifelse(with_tail, round(mean(parameters[["Tail_Nmut_validation"]])), c()),
        vapply(seq_len(N_clusters), function(i) round(mean(parameters[[paste0("Cluster_", i, "_Nmut_validation")]])), numeric(1))
    )
)
cluster_burden
#>   Compartment       CCF nMut nMut_inference_A nMut_inference_B nMut_validation
#> 1        Tail        NA 7875             2718             2139            2389
#> 2   Cluster 1 0.8702543 7299             4252             4252            4252
#> 3   Cluster 2 0.5992796 7552              882              882             882

5. Assign mutations to DECODE compartment

Although DECODE decomposes only subsamples of diploid mutations, the remaining mutations can also be mapped to compartments inferred from DECODE (i.e., mutation clusters and the tail if detected), including those with different copy number states. However, their VAFs must first be converted to be compatible with the copy number state of DECODE’s input mutation table. This requires first inferring mutation multiplicities, which can be performed with GRITIC for instance.

# python rungritic_cmd.py \
#   --mutation_table path/to/mutation_table.tsv \
#   --copy_number_table path/to/cn_table.tsv \
#   --purity 0.87 \
#   --sample_id Example \
#   --output path/to/gritic_output

Once results from GRITIC or other CNA timing algorithms have been ascertained, the multiplicities can be assigned for each mutation. Here we use a pre-annotated mutation table as an example.

NOTE FOR YANJIE: FOR mutation_table_with_multiplicity.csv, START FROM mutation_table.csv THEN ADD COLUMNS nMajor, nMinor and multiplicity. DO NOT ADD COLUMN normalized_VAF.

# mutation_table_with_multiplicity <- read.csv( # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< UNCOMMENT WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
#     system.file("extdata", "mutation_table_with_multiplicity.csv", package = "DECODE")
# )
mutation_table_with_multiplicity <- # <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< REMOVE WHEN DECODE IS PUBLIC
    # read.csv("/Users/kndinh/RESEARCH AND EVERYTHING/Projects/GITHUB/DECODE/inst/extdata/mutation_table_with_multiplicity.csv")
    read.csv("/Users/yanjiechen/Documents/Github/DECODE/inst/extdata/mutation_table_with_multiplicity.csv")
head(mutation_table_with_multiplicity)
#>    Mutation_ID Chromosome  Position Ref_count Alt_count nMajor nMinor
#> 1  mutation_30       chr1   6111125        66        39      2      1
#> 2 mutation_107       chr1  10962862       119         3      1      1
#> 3 mutation_193       chr1  17485594       114         3      1      1
#> 4 mutation_320       chr1  56014346       134        64      2      1
#> 5 mutation_558       chr1 180884386         7        24      1      0
#> 6 mutation_736       chr1 183695959        51         1      1      1
#>   multiplicity
#> 1            2
#> 2            1
#> 3            1
#> 4            2
#> 5            1
#> 6            1

The relationship between a mutation’s VAF and its CCF can be rearranged to calculate each mutation’s normalized VAF, i.e., the VAF if the mutation were diploid: \[f_\text{normalized} = f \cdot \left.\dfrac{\rho}{2\rho + 2(1-\rho)}\middle/\dfrac{m \cdot \rho}{c \cdot \rho + 2(1-\rho)}\right.\] where \(f\) is the mutation’s observed VAF, \(m\) and \(c\) are its multiplicity and total copy number, respectively, and \(\rho\) is the sample purity. If a different copy number state was implemented for DECODE, the fraction in the numerator can be adjusted accordingly.

mutation_table_with_multiplicity$VAF <- with(
    mutation_table_with_multiplicity,
    Alt_count / (Ref_count + Alt_count)
)
mutation_table_with_multiplicity$total_cn <- with(
    mutation_table_with_multiplicity,
    nMajor + nMinor
)
mutation_table_with_multiplicity$normalized_VAF <- with(
    mutation_table_with_multiplicity,
    VAF * purity / (2 * purity + 2 * (1 - purity)) /
        ((multiplicity * purity) / (total_cn * purity + 2 * (1 - purity)))
)
head(mutation_table_with_multiplicity)
#>    Mutation_ID Chromosome  Position Ref_count Alt_count nMajor nMinor
#> 1  mutation_30       chr1   6111125        66        39      2      1
#> 2 mutation_107       chr1  10962862       119         3      1      1
#> 3 mutation_193       chr1  17485594       114         3      1      1
#> 4 mutation_320       chr1  56014346       134        64      2      1
#> 5 mutation_558       chr1 180884386         7        24      1      0
#> 6 mutation_736       chr1 183695959        51         1      1      1
#>   multiplicity        VAF total_cn normalized_VAF
#> 1            2 0.37142857        3     0.26652361
#> 2            1 0.02459016        2     0.02459016
#> 3            1 0.02564103        2     0.02564103
#> 4            2 0.32323232        3     0.23193974
#> 5            1 0.77419355        1     0.43732091
#> 6            1 0.01923077        2     0.01923077

Function DECODE_mutation_assignment() calculates the probability that each mutation belongs to a given DECODE compartment, given the mutation’s normalized VAF. This is sometimes referred to as the probabilistic or soft subclonal mutation assignment. By default, DECODE_mutation_assignment() uses the best DECODE fit for the inference A subsample. The user can change to a different subsample with argument mode (which accepts "inference_A", "inference_B" or "validation") and a different fit by changing arguments with_tail ("TRUE" or "FALSE") and N_clusters (any integer cluster count that DECODE considered).

NOTE FOR YANJIE: Change DECODE_plot_SFS() to default mode = “inference_A”, with_tail = NA, N_clusters = NULL

mutation_assignment <- DECODE_mutation_assignment(
    DECODE_result = DECODE_result,
    mutation_table = mutation_table_with_multiplicity
)
#> 
#> Assign mutations to DECODE components with configuration with tail + 2 clusters...
head(mutation_assignment)
#>    Mutation_ID Chromosome  Position Ref_count Alt_count nMajor nMinor
#> 1  mutation_30       chr1   6111125        66        39      2      1
#> 2 mutation_107       chr1  10962862       119         3      1      1
#> 3 mutation_193       chr1  17485594       114         3      1      1
#> 4 mutation_320       chr1  56014346       134        64      2      1
#> 5 mutation_558       chr1 180884386         7        24      1      0
#> 6 mutation_736       chr1 183695959        51         1      1      1
#>   multiplicity        VAF total_cn normalized_VAF  prob_tail prob_cluster_1
#> 1            2 0.37142857        3     0.26652361 0.34656924   1.448973e-02
#> 2            1 0.02459016        2     0.02459016 1.00000000   1.383583e-24
#> 3            1 0.02564103        2     0.02564103 1.00000000   1.383583e-24
#> 4            2 0.32323232        3     0.23193974 0.56259591   3.435532e-03
#> 5            1 0.77419355        1     0.43732091 0.02731875   9.686984e-01
#> 6            1 0.01923077        2     0.01923077 1.00000000   2.990559e-38
#>   prob_cluster_2
#> 1   6.389410e-01
#> 2   9.816570e-15
#> 3   9.816570e-15
#> 4   4.339686e-01
#> 5   3.982896e-03
#> 6   1.943856e-23

The columns prob_tail, prob_cluster_1, prob_cluster_2, … contain the probabilities that each mutation belongs to the tail or each cluster, respectively. The mutations in VAF bins that DECODE did not decompose, e.g., due to lack of data in the input diploid mutation table, are assigned NA probabilities.

If the deterministic (or hard) assignment is preferred, each mutation can be assigned to the compartment with the highest probability, or the compartment with probability higher than a specified threshold (e.g., 0.6 or 0.7).
